items.ed: 'A lot more interesting' ist richtig. Verwende 'a lot', 'much' oder 'far' vor Komparativen, niemals 'very'.

items.p: There are ___ students in our class than in theirs.
a: fewer
items.h: 'Students' is countable (you can count them). Do you use 'fewer' or 'less' with countable nouns?
items.hd: 'Students' ist zählbar. Verwendet man 'fewer' oder 'less' bei zählbaren Nomen?
items.e: Use 'fewer' with countable nouns (fewer students) and 'less' with uncountable nouns (less water).
items.ed: Verwende 'fewer' bei zählbaren Nomen (fewer students) und 'less' bei nicht zählbaren Nomen (less water).

items.p: We should drink ___ sugar in our tea.
a: less
items.h: Can you count 'sugar'? No — it's uncountable. Which word goes with uncountable nouns?
items.hd: Kann man 'sugar' zählen? Nein — es ist nicht zählbar. Welches Wort passt zu nicht zählbaren Nomen?
items.e: 'Sugar' is uncountable, so use 'less'. 'Fewer' is only for countable nouns (fewer apples).
items.ed: 'Sugar' ist nicht zählbar, daher verwende 'less'. 'Fewer' ist nur für zählbare Nomen (fewer apples).

items.p: My new phone is ___ better than my old one.
a: far
items.h: 'Far' is a strong intensifier for comparatives. 'Very', 'so', and 'too' don't work before comparatives.
items.hd: 'Far' ist ein starker Verstärker für Komparative. 'Very', 'so' und 'too' funktionieren nicht vor Komparativen.
items.e: 'Far better' means 'much better'. Use 'far', 'much', 'a lot', or 'even' before comparatives.
items.ed: 'Far better' bedeutet 'viel besser'. Verwende 'far', 'much', 'a lot' oder 'even' vor Komparativen.

items.p: After the training, she ran ___ faster than before.
a: even
items.h: Which word means 'surprisingly more' before a comparative? Think: 'even faster'.
items.hd: Welches Wort bedeutet 'überraschend mehr' vor einem Komparativ? Denke: 'even faster'.
items.e: 'Even' before a comparative adds surprise or emphasis: 'even faster' = sogar noch schneller.
items.ed: 'Even' vor einem Komparativ drückt Überraschung oder Betonung aus: 'even faster' = sogar noch schneller.

items.p: Which sentence is correct?
items.c: This book is much more exciting than that one.
a: This book is much more exciting than that one.
ds: This book is very more exciting than that one.
This book is more much exciting than that one.
This book is much more excitinger than that one.
items.h: Remember: 'much' or 'a lot' goes BEFORE 'more + adjective'. And never use 'very' with comparatives.
items.hd: Merke: 'much' oder 'a lot' steht VOR 'more + Adjektiv'. Und verwende nie 'very' mit Komparativen.
items.e: 'Much more exciting' is correct. The intensifier 'much' goes before the comparative 'more exciting'.
items.ed: 'Much more exciting' ist richtig. Der Verstärker 'much' steht vor dem Komparativ 'more exciting'.

items.p: Choose the correct sentence:
items.c: There are fewer cars on the road today.
a: There are fewer cars on the road today.
ds: There are less cars on the road today.
There are more fewer cars on the road today.
There are fewest cars on the road today.
items.h: 'Cars' is countable. Which word do we use for 'a smaller number of' countable things?
items.hd: 'Cars' ist zählbar. Welches Wort verwenden wir für 'eine geringere Anzahl von' zählbaren Dingen?
items.e: 'Fewer' is for countable nouns (fewer cars). 'Less' is for uncountable nouns (less traffic).
items.ed: 'Fewer' steht bei zählbaren Nomen (fewer cars). 'Less' steht bei nicht zählbaren Nomen (less traffic).

items.p: Which sentence uses 'fewer' and 'less' correctly?
items.c: You should eat less sugar and fewer sweets.
a: You should eat less sugar and fewer sweets.
ds: You should eat fewer sugar and less sweets.
You should eat less sugar and less sweets.
You should eat fewer sugar and fewer sweets.
items.h: Sugar = uncountable (use 'less'). Sweets = countable (use 'fewer'). Check both parts.
items.hd: Sugar = nicht zählbar ('less'). Sweets = zählbar ('fewer'). Prüfe beide Teile.
items.e: 'Less' goes with uncountable nouns (sugar). 'Fewer' goes with countable nouns (sweets). Both must be correct.
items.ed: 'Less' steht bei nicht zählbaren Nomen (sugar). 'Fewer' steht bei zählbaren Nomen (sweets). Beides muss stimmen.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: My sister is very taller than me.
items.c: My sister is much taller than me.
a: My sister is much taller than me.
My sister is a lot taller than me.
My sister is far taller than me.
items.h: 'Very' doesn't work before comparatives. What word should replace 'very'?
items.hd: 'Very' funktioniert nicht vor Komparativen. Welches Wort sollte 'very' ersetzen?
items.e: You cannot use 'very' with comparatives. Use 'much', 'a lot', or 'far' instead: 'much taller'.
items.ed: Man kann 'very' nicht mit Komparativen verwenden. Verwende stattdessen 'much', 'a lot' oder 'far': 'much taller'.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: There is less apples in this box.
items.c: There are fewer apples in this box.
a: There are fewer apples in this box.
There are less apples in this box.
There are fewer apples in this box
items.h: 'Apples' is countable. Which word should you use: 'fewer' or 'less'? Also check the verb.
items.hd: 'Apples' ist zählbar. Welches Wort solltest du verwenden: 'fewer' oder 'less'? Prüfe auch das Verb.
items.e: Use 'fewer' (not 'less') with countable nouns like 'apples'. Also, 'there are' (not 'there is') for plural.
items.ed: Verwende 'fewer' (nicht 'less') bei zählbaren Nomen wie 'apples'. Außerdem: 'there are' (nicht 'there is') für die Mehrzahl.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: He is more faster than his brother.
items.c: He is much faster than his brother.
a: He is much faster than his brother.
He is a lot faster than his brother.
He is far faster than his brother.
He is faster than his brother.
items.h: 'Faster' is already a comparative. You don't need 'more' before it. What word can you use instead?
items.hd: 'Faster' ist bereits ein Komparativ. Du brauchst 'more' nicht davor. Welches Wort kannst du stattdessen verwenden?
items.e: 'Faster' is already comparative. Don't use 'more' with '-er' forms. Use 'much', 'a lot', or 'far' to intensify.
items.ed: 'Faster' ist bereits ein Komparativ. Verwende 'more' nicht mit '-er'-Formen. Verwende 'much', 'a lot' oder 'far' zur Verstärkung.

items.p: You're shopping for a phone with a friend. The iPhone costs 1200 EUR and the Samsung costs 400 EUR. Emphasise the difference: 'The iPhone is ________ more expensive than the Samsung.'
a: much
a lot
far
items.h: The price difference is very large (800 EUR). Which intensifier shows a big difference?
items.hd: Der Preisunterschied ist sehr groß (800 EUR). Welcher Verstärker zeigt einen großen Unterschied?
items.e: Use 'much', 'a lot', or 'far' before comparatives to emphasise a large difference.
items.ed: Verwende 'much', 'a lot' oder 'far' vor Komparativen, um einen großen Unterschied zu betonen.

items.p: You're describing your family to a new friend. Your brother is 175 cm and you're 172 cm. It's a small difference. Tell your friend: 'My brother is ________ taller than me.'
a: a bit
slightly
a little
items.h: The height difference is only 3 cm. Which intensifier shows a small difference?
items.hd: Der Größenunterschied beträgt nur 3 cm. Welcher Verstärker zeigt einen kleinen Unterschied?
items.e: Use 'a bit', 'slightly', or 'a little' before comparatives to show a small difference.
items.ed: Verwende 'a bit', 'slightly' oder 'a little' vor Komparativen, um einen kleinen Unterschied zu zeigen.

items.p: After a maths test, your classmate asks how it was compared to the last one. You got 95% this time vs. 60% last time. Complete: 'It was ________ easier than the last one!'
a: much
a lot
far
items.h: The score jumped from 60% to 95% — that's a huge difference. Which intensifier matches?
items.hd: Die Note sprang von 60% auf 95% — das ist ein riesiger Unterschied. Welcher Verstärker passt?
items.e: A large difference (35 percentage points) calls for a strong intensifier: much, a lot, or far.
items.ed: Ein großer Unterschied (35 Prozentpunkte) erfordert einen starken Verstärker: much, a lot oder far.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Mein Bruder ist viel größer als ich.
items.c: My brother is much taller than me.
a: My brother is much taller than me.
My brother is a lot taller than me.
My brother is much taller than I am.
My brother is far taller than me.
items.h: 'Viel' before a comparative = 'much', 'a lot', or 'far'. 'Größer' = taller.
items.hd: 'Viel' vor einem Komparativ = 'much', 'a lot' oder 'far'. 'Größer' = taller.
items.e: German 'viel größer' = 'much taller'. Use 'much', 'a lot', or 'far' (never 'very') before comparatives.
items.ed: Deutsch 'viel größer' = 'much taller'. Verwende 'much', 'a lot' oder 'far' (nie 'very') vor Komparativen.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Wir haben weniger Hausaufgaben als letzte Woche.
items.c: We have less homework than last week.
a: We have less homework than last week.
We've got less homework than last week.
We have less homework than last week
We've got less homework than last week
items.h: 'Hausaufgaben' is uncountable in English ('homework'). Use 'less' for uncountable nouns.
items.hd: 'Hausaufgaben' ist im Englischen nicht zählbar ('homework'). Verwende 'less' für nicht zählbare Nomen.
items.e: 'Homework' is uncountable in English (even though 'Hausaufgaben' is plural in German), so use 'less homework'.
items.ed: 'Homework' ist im Englischen nicht zählbar (obwohl 'Hausaufgaben' im Deutschen Mehrzahl ist), daher 'less homework'.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: taller / is / brother / much / my / than / me
items.c: My brother is much taller than me.
a: My brother is much taller than me.
My brother is much taller than me
items.h: Start with the subject (My brother), then the verb (is), then the intensified comparative.
items.hd: Beginne mit dem Subjekt (My brother), dann das Verb (is), dann der verstärkte Komparativ.
items.e: The word order is: Subject + is + intensifier (much) + comparative (taller) + than + object.
items.ed: Die Wortstellung ist: Subjekt + is + Verstärker (much) + Komparativ (taller) + than + Objekt.

items.p: Match each noun with the correct word (fewer or less). 1: ___ homework 2: ___ friends 3: ___ money 4: ___ books 5: ___ time
items.h: Countable nouns (you can count them) → fewer. Uncountable nouns → less.
items.hd: Zählbare Nomen → fewer. Nicht zählbare Nomen → less.
items.e: Fewer + countable (friends, books). Less + uncountable (homework, money, time).
items.ed: Fewer + zählbar (friends, books). Less + nicht zählbar (homework, money, time).

items.p: Form a question: 'London is much bigger than Vienna.' → Ask about London. Start with 'Is'.
items.c: Is London much bigger than Vienna?
a: Is London much bigger than Vienna?
Is London much bigger than Vienna
items.h: For yes/no questions with 'is', move 'is' to the front. Keep 'much bigger' together.
items.hd: Für Ja/Nein-Fragen mit 'is' stelle 'is' nach vorne. Lass 'much bigger' zusammen.
items.e: To form a yes/no question, move 'is' before the subject: 'Is London much bigger than Vienna?'
items.ed: Um eine Ja/Nein-Frage zu bilden, stelle 'is' vor das Subjekt: 'Is London much bigger than Vienna?'

items.p: If it ___ (rain), we will stay at home.
a: rains
items.h: In the if-clause of a first conditional, use the present simple — NOT 'will'.
items.hd: Im if-Satz des First Conditional verwendet man das Present Simple — NICHT 'will'.
items.e: First conditional: if + present simple, will + base form. 'If it rains, we will stay at home.'
items.ed: First Conditional: if + Present Simple, will + Grundform. 'If it rains, we will stay at home.'

items.p: If you study hard, you ___ pass the test.
a: will
'll
items.h: The result clause in a first conditional uses 'will' + base form.
items.hd: Der Ergebnissatz im First Conditional verwendet 'will' + Grundform.
items.e: First conditional: if + present simple, WILL + base form. The main clause needs 'will'.
items.ed: First Conditional: if + Present Simple, WILL + Grundform. Der Hauptsatz braucht 'will'.

items.p: We ___ (go) to the park if the weather is nice.
a: will go
'll go
items.h: This is a first conditional. The main clause (result) uses 'will' + base verb.
items.hd: Das ist ein First Conditional. Der Hauptsatz (Ergebnis) verwendet 'will' + Grundform.
items.e: In first conditional, the main clause uses 'will + base form': 'We will go to the park if the weather is nice.'
items.ed: Im First Conditional verwendet der Hauptsatz 'will + Grundform': 'We will go to the park if the weather is nice.'

items.p: If she ___ (not / hurry), she will miss the bus.
a: doesn't hurry
does not hurry
items.h: In the if-clause, use present simple negative. 'She' = 3rd person → doesn't + base form.
items.hd: Im if-Satz verwendet man die verneinte Gegenwartsform. 'She' = 3. Person → doesn't + Grundform.
items.e: If-clause needs present simple: 'If she doesn't hurry' (not 'won't hurry'). 3rd person uses 'doesn't'.
items.ed: Der if-Satz braucht Present Simple: 'If she doesn't hurry' (nicht 'won't hurry'). 3. Person verwendet 'doesn't'.

items.p: If I ___ (have) enough money, I will buy a new skateboard.
a: have
items.h: Is this about a real possibility? Then it's first conditional: if + present simple.
items.hd: Geht es um eine echte Möglichkeit? Dann ist es First Conditional: if + Present Simple.
items.e: First conditional for real/possible future: if + present simple. 'If I have enough money' (not 'will have' or 'had').
items.ed: First Conditional für reale/mögliche Zukunft: if + Present Simple. 'If I have enough money' (nicht 'will have' oder 'had').

items.p: If you ___ (not / eat) breakfast, you won't have energy for PE class.
a: don't eat
do not eat
items.h: Remember: NO 'will' in the if-clause. Use present simple negative: don't + base form.
items.hd: Merke: KEIN 'will' im if-Satz. Verwende die verneinte Gegenwartsform: don't + Grundform.
items.e: First conditional if-clause: present simple negative. 'If you don't eat' (NOT 'won't eat').
items.ed: First Conditional if-Satz: Present Simple verneinend. 'If you don't eat' (NICHT 'won't eat').

items.p: Which sentence is correct?
items.c: If it snows tomorrow, we will build a snowman.
a: If it snows tomorrow, we will build a snowman.
ds: If it will snow tomorrow, we will build a snowman.
If it snows tomorrow, we build a snowman.
If it snowed tomorrow, we will build a snowman.
items.h: First conditional: if + present simple, will + base form. Check both clauses.
items.hd: First Conditional: if + Present Simple, will + Grundform. Prüfe beide Satzteile.
items.e: Correct first conditional: 'If it snows (present simple), we will build (will + base).'
items.ed: Richtiges First Conditional: 'If it snows (Present Simple), we will build (will + Grundform).'

items.p: Choose the correct sentence:
items.c: I won't go to the party if I don't feel better.
a: I won't go to the party if I don't feel better.
ds: I won't go to the party if I won't feel better.
I don't go to the party if I don't feel better.
I won't go to the party if I didn't feel better.
items.h: The if-clause needs present simple (don't feel). The main clause needs will/won't.
items.hd: Der if-Satz braucht Present Simple (don't feel). Der Hauptsatz braucht will/won't.
items.e: 'If I don't feel better' (present simple in if-clause) + 'I won't go' (will in main clause).
items.ed: 'If I don't feel better' (Present Simple im if-Satz) + 'I won't go' (will im Hauptsatz).

items.p: Choose the correct first conditional sentence:
items.c: If you eat too much, you'll feel sick.
a: If you eat too much, you'll feel sick.
ds: If you will eat too much, you feel sick.
If you ate too much, you'll feel sick.
If you eat too much, you would feel sick.
items.h: First conditional: if + present simple, ... will + infinitive. Check both clauses carefully.
items.hd: First conditional: if + present simple, ... will + Infinitiv. Überprüfe beide Satzteile sorgfältig.
items.e: First conditional uses present simple in the if-clause and will + infinitive in the main clause: 'If you eat too much, you'll feel sick.'
items.ed: First conditional verwendet present simple im if-Satz und will + Infinitiv im Hauptsatz: 'If you eat too much, you'll feel sick.'

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: If it will rain, we will stay inside.
items.c: If it rains, we will stay inside.
a: If it rains, we will stay inside.
If it rains we will stay inside.
If it rains, we will stay inside
items.h: Look at the if-clause. Should 'will' be there?
items.hd: Schau dir den if-Satz an. Sollte 'will' dort stehen?
items.e: No 'will' in the if-clause! Use present simple: 'If it rains' (not 'if it will rain').
items.ed: Kein 'will' im if-Satz! Verwende Present Simple: 'If it rains' (nicht 'if it will rain').

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: If you eat too much, you feel sick.
items.c: If you eat too much, you will feel sick.
a: If you eat too much, you will feel sick.
If you eat too much, you'll feel sick.
If you eat too much, you will feel sick
items.h: The if-clause is correct (present simple). But what's missing in the main clause?
items.hd: Der if-Satz ist richtig (Present Simple). Aber was fehlt im Hauptsatz?
items.e: The main clause needs 'will': 'you will feel sick'. Present tense in both clauses is wrong in first conditional.
items.ed: Der Hauptsatz braucht 'will': 'you will feel sick'. Present in beiden Satzteilen ist beim First Conditional falsch.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: If she would study more, she will get better marks.
items.c: If she studies more, she will get better marks.
a: If she studies more, she will get better marks.
If she studies more, she will get better marks
If she studies more, she'll get better marks.
items.h: 'Would' in the if-clause is wrong for first conditional. What tense belongs there?
items.hd: 'Would' im if-Satz ist falsch für das First Conditional. Welche Zeitform gehört dort hin?
items.e: First conditional uses present simple in the if-clause: 'If she studies' (not 'would study').
items.ed: Das First Conditional verwendet Present Simple im if-Satz: 'If she studies' (nicht 'would study').

items.p: Your younger brother is worried about his test tomorrow. Encourage him: 'If you ________ (study) hard, you ________ (pass) the test.'
a: study ... will pass
study ... 'll pass
study, will pass
items.h: First conditional: if + present simple, ... will + infinitive. The if-clause uses present simple, not future.
items.hd: First conditional: if + present simple, ... will + Infinitiv. Der if-Satz verwendet present simple, nicht Zukunft.
items.e: First conditional: 'If you study' (present simple in the if-clause) + 'you will pass' (will + infinitive in the result clause).
items.ed: First conditional: 'If you study' (present simple im if-Satz) + 'you will pass' (will + Infinitiv im Ergebnissatz).

items.p: You're looking out the window at grey clouds with your little cousin. Make a plan: 'If it ________ (snow), we ________ (make) a snowman!'
a: snows ... will make
snows ... 'll make
snows, will make
items.h: First conditional: if + present simple (with -s for third person), ... will + infinitive.
items.hd: First conditional: if + present simple (mit -s für dritte Person), ... will + Infinitiv.
items.e: 'If it snows' uses present simple (third person -s). 'We will make' uses will + infinitive for the result.
items.ed: 'If it snows' verwendet present simple (dritte Person -s). 'We will make' verwendet will + Infinitiv für das Ergebnis.

items.p: Your sister is still getting ready and the bus leaves in 5 minutes. Complete the warning: 'If she ________ (not hurry), she ________ (miss) the bus!'
a: doesn't hurry ... will miss
doesn't hurry ... 'll miss
does not hurry ... will miss
items.h: Negative first conditional: if + don't/doesn't + base form, ... will + infinitive.
items.hd: Negatives First Conditional: if + don't/doesn't + Grundform, ... will + Infinitiv.
items.e: 'If she doesn't hurry' (negative present simple) + 'she will miss' (will + infinitive). Don't use 'won't' in the if-clause.
items.ed: 'If she doesn't hurry' (negatives present simple) + 'she will miss' (will + Infinitiv). Verwende nicht 'won't' im if-Satz.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Wenn es morgen regnet, werden wir zu Hause bleiben.
items.c: If it rains tomorrow, we will stay at home.
a: If it rains tomorrow, we will stay at home.
If it rains tomorrow, we'll stay at home.
If it rains tomorrow we will stay at home.
We will stay at home if it rains tomorrow.
items.h: 'Wenn' = 'if'. Remember: in the if-clause, use present simple (NOT 'will rain').
items.hd: 'Wenn' = 'if'. Merke: im if-Satz verwende Present Simple (NICHT 'will rain').
items.e: German uses present or future in both parts. In English first conditional: if + present simple, will + base form.
items.ed: Deutsch verwendet Gegenwart oder Zukunft in beiden Teilen. Im englischen First Conditional: if + Present Simple, will + Grundform.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Wenn du nicht bald kommst, gehe ich ohne dich.
items.c: If you don't come soon, I will go without you.
a: If you don't come soon, I will go without you.
If you don't come soon, I'll go without you.
If you do not come soon, I will go without you.
I will go without you if you don't come soon.
items.h: 'Wenn du nicht kommst' = 'If you don't come'. The main clause needs 'will'.
items.hd: 'Wenn du nicht kommst' = 'If you don't come'. Der Hauptsatz braucht 'will'.
items.e: German uses present ('gehe ich'), but English needs 'will' in the main clause: 'I will go without you.'
items.ed: Deutsch verwendet Gegenwart ('gehe ich'), aber Englisch braucht 'will' im Hauptsatz: 'I will go without you.'

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: miss / the bus / if / hurry / you / will / don't / you
items.c: If you don't hurry, you will miss the bus.
a: If you don't hurry, you will miss the bus.
If you don't hurry you will miss the bus.
You will miss the bus if you don't hurry.
items.h: Start with 'If you don't hurry' (the condition), then add the result with 'will'.
items.hd: Beginne mit 'If you don't hurry' (die Bedingung), dann füge das Ergebnis mit 'will' hinzu.
items.e: First conditional word order: If + subject + present simple, subject + will + base form.
items.ed: First Conditional Wortstellung: If + Subjekt + Present Simple, Subjekt + will + Grundform.

items.p: Match each if-clause with the correct result. 1: If it rains, 2: If you don't study, 3: If we hurry, 4: If she calls me,
ds: a: you will fail the test.
b: I will tell her the news.
d: we will catch the train.
items.h: Think about what logically follows each condition. All results use 'will + base form'.
items.hd: Überlege, was logisch auf jede Bedingung folgt. Alle Ergebnisse verwenden 'will + Grundform'.
items.e: 1→c (rain → get wet), 2→a (don't study → fail), 3→d (hurry → catch train), 4→b (calls → tell news).
items.ed: 1→c (Regen → nass werden), 2→a (nicht lernen → durchfallen), 3→d (sich beeilen → Zug erwischen), 4→b (anrufen → Neuigkeiten erzählen).

items.p: Your friend says: 'If it rains tomorrow, we'll stay home.' Is this about a real or imaginary situation? Which sentence type fits?
items.c: First conditional — it's a real possibility for tomorrow.
a: First conditional — it's a real possibility for tomorrow.
ds: Second conditional — it's an imaginary situation that probably won't happen.
Zero conditional — it's a general fact that is always true.
Past conditional — it's about something that already happened.
items.h: The sentence talks about something that might really happen tomorrow. Which conditional is for real, possible future situations?
items.hd: Der Satz spricht über etwas, das morgen wirklich passieren könnte. Welches Conditional ist für reale, mögliche zukünftige Situationen?
items.e: First conditional (if + present simple, will + infinitive) expresses real, possible future situations. Rain tomorrow is a realistic possibility.
items.ed: First conditional (if + present simple, will + Infinitiv) drückt reale, mögliche zukünftige Situationen aus. Regen morgen ist eine realistische Möglichkeit.

items.p: I won't go to the party ___ you come with me.
a: unless
items.h: 'Unless' means 'if...not' or 'except if'. Which word fits: 'I won't go ___ you come'?
items.hd: 'Unless' bedeutet 'wenn...nicht' oder 'es sei denn'. Welches Wort passt?
items.e: 'Unless you come' = 'if you don't come'. 'Unless' means 'except if' or 'if...not'.
items.ed: 'Unless you come' = 'if you don't come'. 'Unless' bedeutet 'es sei denn' oder 'wenn...nicht'.

items.p: You will be late ___ you leave now.
a: unless
items.h: The sentence means: 'You will be late if you DON'T leave now.' Which single word replaces 'if...not'?
items.hd: Der Satz bedeutet: 'Du wirst zu spät kommen, wenn du NICHT jetzt gehst.' Welches Wort ersetzt 'wenn...nicht'?
items.e: 'Unless you leave now' = 'if you don't leave now'. Both mean the same thing.
items.ed: 'Unless you leave now' = 'wenn du nicht jetzt gehst'. Beides bedeutet dasselbe.

items.p: We will have the picnic ___ it rains.
a: unless
items.h: We WILL have the picnic — but only if it DOESN'T rain. Which word means 'if it doesn't'?
items.hd: Wir WERDEN das Picknick machen — aber nur wenn es NICHT regnet. Welches Wort bedeutet 'wenn es nicht'?
items.e: 'Unless it rains' = 'if it doesn't rain'. The picnic will happen except if it rains.
items.ed: 'Unless it rains' = 'wenn es nicht regnet'. Das Picknick findet statt, es sei denn es regnet.

items.p: She won't pass the exam ___ she studies harder.
a: unless
items.h: The meaning is: She won't pass if she DOESN'T study harder. One word for 'if not'?
items.hd: Die Bedeutung ist: Sie wird nicht bestehen, wenn sie NICHT mehr lernt. Ein Wort für 'wenn nicht'?
items.e: 'Unless she studies harder' = 'if she doesn't study harder'. Note: no double negative with 'unless'.
items.ed: 'Unless she studies harder' = 'wenn sie nicht mehr lernt'. Beachte: keine doppelte Verneinung mit 'unless'.

items.p: The match will be cancelled ___ the rain stops.
a: unless
items.h: The match WILL be cancelled — except if the rain stops. One word for 'except if'?
items.hd: Das Spiel WIRD abgesagt — außer wenn der Regen aufhört. Ein Wort für 'außer wenn'?
items.e: 'Unless the rain stops' = 'if the rain doesn't stop' = 'except if the rain stops'.
items.ed: 'Unless the rain stops' = 'wenn der Regen nicht aufhört' = 'außer wenn der Regen aufhört'.

items.p: I'll lend you my book ___ you promise to give it back.
a: if
items.h: Think about the meaning: will the speaker lend the book if the person promises? Or unless they promise?
items.hd: Denke über die Bedeutung nach: Verleiht der Sprecher das Buch, wenn die Person verspricht? Oder es sei denn?
items.e: Here 'if' is correct: the speaker will lend the book IF you promise. 'Unless' would reverse the meaning.
items.ed: Hier ist 'if' richtig: Der Sprecher leiht das Buch, WENN du versprichst. 'Unless' würde die Bedeutung umkehren.

items.p: Which sentence means the same as 'I won't help you if you don't ask me'?
items.c: I won't help you unless you ask me.
a: I won't help you unless you ask me.
ds: I won't help you unless you don't ask me.
I will help you unless you ask me.
I won't help you if you ask me.
items.h: 'Unless' = 'if...not'. So 'unless you ask' = 'if you don't ask'. Watch out for double negatives!
items.hd: 'Unless' = 'wenn...nicht'. Also 'unless you ask' = 'wenn du nicht fragst'. Achtung bei doppelter Verneinung!
items.e: 'Unless you ask me' = 'if you don't ask me'. Don't use 'don't' after 'unless' — that's a double negative.
items.ed: 'Unless you ask me' = 'wenn du mich nicht fragst'. Verwende kein 'don't' nach 'unless' — das wäre eine doppelte Verneinung.

items.p: Choose the correct sentence:
items.c: We won't go on the trip unless the weather gets better.
a: We won't go on the trip unless the weather gets better.
ds: We won't go on the trip unless the weather doesn't get better.
We won't go on the trip unless the weather will get better.
We don't go on the trip unless the weather gets better.
items.h: No double negative with 'unless'! And no 'will' in the unless-clause.
items.hd: Keine doppelte Verneinung mit 'unless'! Und kein 'will' im unless-Satz.
items.e: 'Unless' already contains the negative. Don't add 'not/don't/doesn't' after it. Also, no 'will' in the unless-clause.
items.ed: 'Unless' enthält bereits die Verneinung. Füge kein 'not/don't/doesn't' danach hinzu. Auch kein 'will' im unless-Satz.

items.p: Which two sentences mean the SAME thing?
items.c: 'Unless he apologises, I won't talk to him.' = 'If he doesn't apologise, I won't talk to him.'
a: 'Unless he apologises, I won't talk to him.' = 'If he doesn't apologise, I won't talk to him.'
ds: 'Unless he apologises, I won't talk to him.' = 'If he apologises, I won't talk to him.'
'Unless he doesn't apologise, I won't talk to him.' = 'If he doesn't apologise, I won't talk to him.'
'Unless he apologises, I will talk to him.' = 'If he doesn't apologise, I won't talk to him.'
items.h: 'Unless he apologises' = 'If he doesn't apologise'. Both the condition and result must match.
items.hd: 'Unless he apologises' = 'If he doesn't apologise'. Sowohl Bedingung als auch Ergebnis müssen übereinstimmen.
items.e: 'Unless' = 'if...not'. So 'Unless he apologises, I won't talk to him' = 'If he doesn't apologise, I won't talk to him.'
items.ed: 'Unless' = 'wenn...nicht'. Also 'Unless he apologises, I won't talk to him' = 'Wenn er sich nicht entschuldigt, werde ich nicht mit ihm reden.'

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: I won't go unless you don't come with me.
items.c: I won't go unless you come with me.
a: I won't go unless you come with me.
I won't go unless you come with me
I will not go unless you come with me.
items.h: 'Unless' already means 'if not'. Adding 'don't' creates a double negative!
items.hd: 'Unless' bedeutet bereits 'wenn nicht'. 'Don't' hinzuzufügen erzeugt eine doppelte Verneinung!
items.e: 'Unless' = 'if...not'. So 'unless you come' = 'if you don't come'. 'Unless you don't come' is a double negative.
items.ed: 'Unless' = 'wenn...nicht'. Also 'unless you come' = 'wenn du nicht kommst'. 'Unless you don't come' ist eine doppelte Verneinung.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: We will be late unless we will hurry.
items.c: We will be late unless we hurry.
a: We will be late unless we hurry.
We will be late unless we hurry
We'll be late unless we hurry.
items.h: The unless-clause follows the same rule as the if-clause: no 'will'!
items.hd: Der unless-Satz folgt der gleichen Regel wie der if-Satz: kein 'will'!
items.e: Like the if-clause, the unless-clause uses present simple: 'unless we hurry' (not 'unless we will hurry').
items.ed: Wie der if-Satz verwendet der unless-Satz Present Simple: 'unless we hurry' (nicht 'unless we will hurry').

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: Unless you don't finish your homework, you can't play outside.
items.c: Unless you finish your homework, you can't play outside.
a: Unless you finish your homework, you can't play outside.
Unless you finish your homework you can't play outside.
If you don't finish your homework, you can't play outside.
items.h: 'Unless' already means 'if not'. Remove the extra 'don't' to fix the double negative.
items.hd: 'Unless' bedeutet bereits 'wenn nicht'. Entferne das zusätzliche 'don't', um die doppelte Verneinung zu beheben.
items.e: 'Unless you finish' = 'if you don't finish'. 'Unless you don't finish' is a double negative and means 'if you DO finish'.
items.ed: 'Unless you finish' = 'wenn du nicht fertig wirst'. 'Unless you don't finish' ist eine doppelte Verneinung und bedeutet 'wenn du fertig wirst'.

items.p: Your friend is still on the sofa and school starts in 10 minutes. Warn them: 'Unless you ________ now, you ________ late!'
a: leave ... 'll be
leave ... will be
leave, will be
leave, 'll be
items.h: 'Unless' means 'if ... not'. After 'unless', use the affirmative verb (not 'don't leave').
items.hd: 'Unless' bedeutet 'if ... not'. Nach 'unless' verwende das bejahte Verb (nicht 'don't leave').
items.e: 'Unless you leave' = 'If you don't leave'. After 'unless', the verb is affirmative because the negative meaning is already in 'unless'.
items.ed: 'Unless you leave' = 'If you don't leave'. Nach 'unless' ist das Verb bejahend, weil die Verneinung bereits in 'unless' steckt.

items.p: Your teammate hasn't been coming to basketball practice. Explain the consequence to her: 'Unless you ________ (practise), you ________ (not improve).'
a: practise ... won't improve
practise ... will not improve
practise, won't improve
items.h: 'Unless' takes an affirmative verb. The main clause stays negative because the consequence is negative.
items.hd: 'Unless' steht mit einem bejahten Verb. Der Hauptsatz bleibt negativ, weil die Konsequenz negativ ist.
items.e: 'Unless you practise' (affirmative after unless) + 'you won't improve' (negative consequence in main clause).
items.ed: 'Unless you practise' (bejahend nach unless) + 'you won't improve' (negative Konsequenz im Hauptsatz).

items.p: You're about to leave for a walk and it looks cloudy. Remind your dad: 'Unless we ________ (bring) an umbrella, we ________ (get) wet.'
a: bring ... 'll get
bring ... will get
bring, will get
bring, 'll get
items.h: 'Unless' already contains the negative meaning. Use the affirmative verb after 'unless'.
items.hd: 'Unless' enthält bereits die negative Bedeutung. Verwende das bejahte Verb nach 'unless'.
items.e: 'Unless we bring an umbrella, we'll get wet' = 'If we don't bring an umbrella, we'll get wet.'
items.ed: 'Unless we bring an umbrella, we'll get wet' = 'If we don't bring an umbrella, we'll get wet.'

items.p: 🇩🇪 Ich gehe nicht schwimmen, es sei denn, du kommst mit.
items.c: I won't go swimming unless you come with me.
a: I won't go swimming unless you come with me.
I won't go swimming unless you come along.
I will not go swimming unless you come with me.
Unless you come with me, I won't go swimming.
items.h: 'Es sei denn' = 'unless'. Remember: no 'don't' after 'unless'!
items.hd: 'Es sei denn' = 'unless'. Merke: kein 'don't' nach 'unless'!
items.e: 'Es sei denn' = 'unless'. 'Unless you come' = 'wenn du nicht kommst'. No double negative!
items.ed: 'Es sei denn' = 'unless'. 'Unless you come' = 'wenn du nicht kommst'. Keine doppelte Verneinung!

items.p: 🇩🇪 Du wirst den Test nicht bestehen, wenn du nicht mehr lernst.
items.c: You won't pass the test unless you study more.
a: You won't pass the test unless you study more.
You will not pass the test unless you study more.
Unless you study more, you won't pass the test.
You won't pass the test if you don't study more.
items.h: 'Wenn du nicht' can be translated as 'unless you' (without 'don't') or 'if you don't'.
items.hd: 'Wenn du nicht' kann als 'unless you' (ohne 'don't') oder 'if you don't' übersetzt werden.
items.e: 'Wenn du nicht mehr lernst' = 'unless you study more' or 'if you don't study more'. Both are correct.
items.ed: 'Wenn du nicht mehr lernst' = 'unless you study more' oder 'if you don't study more'. Beides ist richtig.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: won't / unless / I / you / go / come / with me
items.c: I won't go unless you come with me.
a: I won't go unless you come with me.
I won't go unless you come with me
Unless you come with me, I won't go.
items.h: Start with the main clause (I won't go), then add the unless-clause.
items.hd: Beginne mit dem Hauptsatz (I won't go), dann füge den unless-Satz hinzu.
items.e: Main clause + unless + condition: 'I won't go unless you come with me.'
items.ed: Hauptsatz + unless + Bedingung: 'I won't go unless you come with me.'

items.p: Match each 'unless' sentence with its 'if...not' meaning. 1: Unless you hurry, you'll be late. 2: I won't buy it unless it's cheap. 3: Unless it stops raining, we'll stay inside. 4: She'll be angry unless you say sorry.
ds: a: I won't buy it if it isn't cheap.
b: She'll be angry if you don't say sorry.
c: If you don't hurry, you'll be late.
d: If it doesn't stop raining, we'll stay inside.
items.h: 'Unless' = 'if...not'. Match each sentence to its paraphrase with 'if + negative'.
items.hd: 'Unless' = 'wenn...nicht'. Ordne jeden Satz seiner Umschreibung mit 'if + Verneinung' zu.
items.e: Each 'unless' sentence has the same meaning as the 'if...not' version: unless you hurry = if you don't hurry, etc.
items.ed: Jeder 'unless'-Satz hat die gleiche Bedeutung wie die 'if...not'-Version: unless you hurry = if you don't hurry, usw.

items.p: Form a question: 'We won't go unless the weather is nice.' → Ask: 'Will we go if the weather isn't nice?'
items.c: Will we go if the weather isn't nice?
a: Will we go if the weather isn't nice?
Will we go if the weather is not nice?
Will we go if the weather isn't nice
items.h: Change 'unless the weather is nice' to 'if the weather isn't nice' and form a yes/no question.
items.hd: Ändere 'unless the weather is nice' zu 'if the weather isn't nice' und bilde eine Ja/Nein-Frage.
items.e: 'Unless the weather is nice' = 'if the weather isn't nice'. Question: 'Will we go if the weather isn't nice?' (Answer: No!)
items.ed: 'Unless the weather is nice' = 'wenn das Wetter nicht schön ist'. Frage: 'Will we go if the weather isn't nice?' (Antwort: Nein!)

items.p: The boy ___ sits next to me is my best friend.
a: who
that
items.h: The boy is a person. Which relative pronoun do we use for people?
items.hd: Der Junge ist eine Person. Welches Relativpronomen verwenden wir für Personen?
items.e: For people, we use 'who' or 'that'. 'Which' is only for things and animals.
items.ed: Für Personen verwenden wir 'who' oder 'that'. 'Which' ist nur für Dinge und Tiere.

items.p: The cake ___ she made was delicious.
a: which
that
items.h: The cake is a thing. Which relative pronoun do we use for things?
items.hd: Der Kuchen ist eine Sache. Welches Relativpronomen verwenden wir für Sachen?
items.e: For things, we use 'which' or 'that'. 'Who' is only for people.
items.ed: Für Sachen verwenden wir 'which' oder 'that'. 'Who' ist nur für Personen.

items.p: I have a friend ___ father is a famous singer.
a: whose
items.h: The father BELONGS to the friend. Which word shows possession in a relative clause?
items.hd: Der Vater GEHÖRT dem Freund. Welches Wort zeigt Besitz in einem Relativsatz?
items.e: 'Whose' shows possession in relative clauses: my friend's father = a friend whose father. Don't confuse it with 'who's' (= who is).
items.ed: 'Whose' zeigt Besitz in Relativsätzen: der Vater meines Freundes = a friend whose father. Verwechsle es nicht mit 'who's' (= who is).

items.p: The teacher ___ helped us with the project was really kind.
a: who
that
items.h: The teacher is a person AND is the subject of 'helped us'. Which pronoun fits?
items.hd: Der Lehrer ist eine Person UND ist das Subjekt von 'helped us'. Welches Pronomen passt?
items.e: 'Who' or 'that' is used for people as the subject of the relative clause. The teacher helped us → who helped us.
items.ed: 'Who' oder 'that' wird für Personen als Subjekt des Relativsatzes verwendet. The teacher helped us → who helped us.

items.p: The film ___ we watched last night was really scary.
a: that
which
items.h: A film is a thing. We can use 'that' or 'which' for things.
items.hd: Ein Film ist eine Sache. Wir können 'that' oder 'which' für Sachen verwenden.
items.e: For things, use 'that' or 'which'. The film which/that we watched. Never use 'what' as a relative pronoun.
items.ed: Für Sachen verwende 'that' oder 'which'. The film which/that we watched. Verwende niemals 'what' als Relativpronomen.

items.p: That is the girl ___ bike was stolen last week.
a: whose
items.h: The bike belongs to the girl. You need a word that shows possession.
items.hd: Das Fahrrad gehört dem Mädchen. Du brauchst ein Wort, das Besitz zeigt.
items.e: 'Whose' replaces 'her' in the relative clause: her bike was stolen → whose bike was stolen.
items.ed: 'Whose' ersetzt 'her' im Relativsatz: her bike was stolen → whose bike was stolen.

items.p: Which sentence is correct?
items.c: The dog which lives next door is very friendly.
a: The dog which lives next door is very friendly.
ds: The dog who lives next door is very friendly.
The dog what lives next door is very friendly.
The dog whose lives next door is very friendly.
items.h: A dog is an animal, not a person. Which relative pronoun fits?
items.hd: Ein Hund ist ein Tier, keine Person. Welches Relativpronomen passt?
items.e: For animals and things, use 'which' or 'that'. 'Who' is for people, 'what' is never a relative pronoun, and 'whose' shows possession.
items.ed: Für Tiere und Dinge verwende 'which' oder 'that'. 'Who' ist für Personen, 'what' ist kein Relativpronomen, und 'whose' zeigt Besitz.

items.p: Which sentence is correct?
items.c: The woman whose car broke down called for help.
a: The woman whose car broke down called for help.
ds: The woman who's car broke down called for help.
The woman which car broke down called for help.
The woman who car broke down called for help.
items.h: The car belongs to the woman. Which relative pronoun shows possession?
items.hd: Das Auto gehört der Frau. Welches Relativpronomen zeigt Besitz?
items.e: 'Whose' is the possessive relative pronoun. Her car broke down → the woman whose car broke down.
items.ed: 'Whose' ist das possessive Relativpronomen. Her car broke down → the woman whose car broke down.

items.p: Which sentence uses the correct relative pronoun?
items.c: I know a girl who can play five instruments.
a: I know a girl who can play five instruments.
ds: I know a girl which can play five instruments.
I know a girl what can play five instruments.
I know a girl whose can play five instruments.
items.h: A girl is a person. Which relative pronoun introduces the subject of a clause about a person?
items.hd: Ein Mädchen ist eine Person. Welches Relativpronomen leitet den Subjekt-Relativsatz über eine Person ein?
items.e: Use 'who' (or 'that') for people as subject: a girl who can play. 'Which' is for things only.
items.ed: Verwende 'who' (oder 'that') für Personen als Subjekt: a girl who can play. 'Which' ist nur für Sachen.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: The man which helped me was very nice.
items.c: The man who helped me was very nice.
a: The man who helped me was very nice.
The man that helped me was very nice.
The man who helped me was very nice
items.h: Is 'the man' a person or a thing? Check the relative pronoun.
items.hd: Ist 'the man' eine Person oder eine Sache? Prüfe das Relativpronomen.
items.e: 'The man' is a person, so we need 'who' or 'that', not 'which'. 'Which' is only for things and animals.
items.ed: 'The man' ist eine Person, also brauchen wir 'who' oder 'that', nicht 'which'. 'Which' ist nur für Dinge und Tiere.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: That's the house what my grandparents built.
items.c: That's the house that my grandparents built.
a: That's the house that my grandparents built.
That's the house which my grandparents built.
That's the house that my grandparents built
That's the house which my grandparents built
items.h: 'What' is NOT a relative pronoun in English. What should replace it?
items.hd: 'What' ist KEIN Relativpronomen im Englischen. Was sollte es ersetzen?
items.e: 'What' cannot be used as a relative pronoun after a noun. Use 'that' or 'which' for things: the house that/which my grandparents built.
items.ed: 'What' kann nicht als Relativpronomen nach einem Nomen verwendet werden. Verwende 'that' oder 'which' für Dinge: the house that/which my grandparents built.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: The boy who's bag is on the floor should pick it up.
items.c: The boy whose bag is on the floor should pick it up.
a: The boy whose bag is on the floor should pick it up.
The boy whose bag is on the floor should pick it up
The boy, whose bag is on the floor, should pick it up.
items.h: Is 'who's' the same as 'whose'? Think about what each one means.
items.hd: Ist 'who's' dasselbe wie 'whose'? Überlege, was jedes Wort bedeutet.
items.e: 'Who's' = who is. 'Whose' = possessive (belonging to someone). The bag belongs to the boy → whose bag.
items.ed: 'Who's' = who is. 'Whose' = Besitz (gehört jemandem). Die Tasche gehört dem Jungen → whose bag.

items.p: Your Austrian friend is visiting your school. Describe the people you see. Complete: 'That's the boy ________ lives next door.'
a: who
that
items.h: You need a relative pronoun for a person. Which word introduces extra information about 'the boy'?
items.hd: Du brauchst ein Relativpronomen für eine Person. Welches Wort leitet zusätzliche Information über 'the boy' ein?
items.e: 'Who' (or 'that') introduces a defining relative clause about a person. 'That's the boy who lives next door.'
items.ed: 'Who' (oder 'that') leitet einen bestimmenden Relativsatz über eine Person ein. 'That's the boy who lives next door.'

items.p: You're showing your friend your new things. Describe them in one sentence: 'I bought a phone ________ has a great camera.'
a: which
that
items.h: You need a relative pronoun for a thing. Which word introduces extra information about 'a phone'?
items.hd: Du brauchst ein Relativpronomen für eine Sache. Welches Wort leitet zusätzliche Information über 'a phone' ein?
items.e: 'Which' (or 'that') introduces a defining relative clause about a thing. 'A phone which has a great camera.'
items.ed: 'Which' (oder 'that') leitet einen bestimmenden Relativsatz über eine Sache ein. 'A phone which has a great camera.'

items.p: Your pen pal asks about your school. Describe your teachers: 'She's the teacher ________ teaches us English.'
a: who
that
items.h: You need a relative pronoun for a person. 'Who' or 'that' both work for people.
items.hd: Du brauchst ein Relativpronomen für eine Person. 'Who' oder 'that' funktionieren beide für Personen.
items.e: 'Who' (or 'that') is used in defining relative clauses about people. 'She's the teacher who teaches us English.'
items.ed: 'Who' (oder 'that') wird in bestimmenden Relativsätzen über Personen verwendet. 'She's the teacher who teaches us English.'

items.p: 🇩🇪 Das Maedchen, das neben mir sitzt, ist sehr nett.
items.c: The girl who sits next to me is very nice.
a: The girl who sits next to me is very nice.
The girl that sits next to me is very nice.
The girl who sits next to me is very nice
The girl that sits next to me is very nice
items.h: In German we use 'das' for all relative clauses. In English, use 'who' or 'that' for people.
items.hd: Im Deutschen verwenden wir 'das' für alle Relativsätze. Im Englischen verwende 'who' oder 'that' für Personen.
items.e: German 'das' (relative pronoun) = 'who' or 'that' for people in English. Das Maedchen, das... → The girl who...
items.ed: Deutsch 'das' (Relativpronomen) = 'who' oder 'that' für Personen im Englischen. Das Mädchen, das... → The girl who...

items.p: 🇩🇪 Das ist der Junge, dessen Hund immer im Park spielt.
items.c: That is the boy whose dog always plays in the park.
a: That is the boy whose dog always plays in the park.
That's the boy whose dog always plays in the park.
That is the boy whose dog always plays in the park
That's the boy whose dog always plays in the park
items.h: 'Dessen' shows possession. What is the English possessive relative pronoun?
items.hd: 'Dessen' zeigt Besitz. Wie heißt das englische possessive Relativpronomen?
items.e: German 'dessen' = 'whose' in English. It shows that the dog belongs to the boy.
items.ed: Deutsch 'dessen' = 'whose' im Englischen. Es zeigt, dass der Hund dem Jungen gehört.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: who / the / is / woman / lives / that / here
items.c: That is the woman who lives here.
a: That is the woman who lives here.
That is the woman who lives here
items.h: Start with 'That is' + the noun + relative pronoun + verb.
items.hd: Beginne mit 'That is' + Nomen + Relativpronomen + Verb.
items.e: The sentence follows this pattern: That is + the woman + who + lives here. The relative clause comes directly after the noun it describes.
items.ed: Der Satz folgt diesem Muster: That is + the woman + who + lives here. Der Relativsatz kommt direkt nach dem Nomen, das er beschreibt.

items.p:
Match each sentence beginning with the correct relative pronoun and ending:
1. The girl ...
2. The phone ...
3. The boy ...
4. The cat ...
a. whose brother is in my class is very tall.
b. which I found was broken.
c. who won the race is my friend.
d. which sleeps all day is very lazy.
items.h: Check: is the noun a person or a thing? Does the ending show possession?
items.hd: Prüfe: Ist das Nomen eine Person oder eine Sache? Zeigt das Ende Besitz?
items.e: Girl = person → 'who' (c). Phone = thing → 'which' (b). Boy + 'whose brother' = possession (a). Cat = animal → 'which' (d).
items.ed: Girl = Person → 'who' (c). Phone = Sache → 'which' (b). Boy + 'whose brother' = Besitz (a). Cat = Tier → 'which' (d).

items.p: You want to know if the person who called is your friend's mum. Ask a question using a relative clause with 'who'.
items.c: Is the person who called your friend's mum?
a: Is the person who called your friend's mum?
Is the person who called your friend's mum
Is that the person who called?
Was the person who called your friend's mum?
items.h: Start with 'Is the person who...' and complete the question.
items.hd: Beginne mit 'Is the person who...' und vervollständige die Frage.
items.e: Questions with relative clauses: Is/Was + the noun + who/which + verb...? The relative clause stays attached to the noun.
items.ed: Fragen mit Relativsätzen: Is/Was + Nomen + who/which + Verb...? Der Relativsatz bleibt am Nomen.

items.p: I have lived in Vienna ___ ten years.
a: for
items.h: 'Ten years' is a duration (how long), not a specific starting point.
items.hd: 'Zehn Jahre' ist eine Dauer (wie lange), kein bestimmter Anfangspunkt.
items.e: 'For' is used with a duration (a period of time): for ten years, for two hours, for a long time.
items.ed: 'For' wird mit einer Dauer verwendet (ein Zeitraum): for ten years, for two hours, for a long time.

items.p: She has been my best friend ___ primary school.
a: since
items.h: 'Primary school' is a point in time (when it started), not a duration.
items.hd: 'Volksschule' ist ein Zeitpunkt (wann es begann), keine Dauer.
items.e: 'Since' is used with a starting point: since primary school, since 2020, since Monday.
items.ed: 'Since' wird mit einem Anfangspunkt verwendet: since primary school, since 2020, since Monday.

items.p: They have known each other ___ 2018.
a: since
items.h: Is '2018' a duration (how long) or a starting point (when it began)?
items.hd: Ist '2018' eine Dauer (wie lange) oder ein Anfangspunkt (wann es begann)?
items.e: 2018 is a specific point in time (starting point), so we use 'since'. For + duration: for six years.
items.ed: 2018 ist ein bestimmter Zeitpunkt (Anfangspunkt), also verwenden wir 'since'. For + Dauer: for six years.

items.p: We haven't seen our cousins ___ a long time.
a: for
items.h: 'A long time' tells us how long (duration), not when it started.
items.hd: 'Eine lange Zeit' sagt uns wie lange (Dauer), nicht wann es begann.
items.e: 'A long time' is a duration, so we use 'for'. Since = starting point (since Monday, since 2020).
items.ed: 'A long time' ist eine Dauer, also verwenden wir 'for'. Since = Anfangspunkt (since Monday, since 2020).

items.p: He has had that phone ___ last Christmas.
a: since
items.h: 'Last Christmas' is a specific time when something started. Duration or starting point?
items.hd: 'Letztes Weihnachten' ist ein bestimmter Zeitpunkt, wann etwas begann. Dauer oder Anfangspunkt?
items.e: 'Last Christmas' is a starting point → since. If it were a duration (e.g. 'three months'), use 'for'.
items.ed: 'Letztes Weihnachten' ist ein Anfangspunkt → since. Wenn es eine Dauer wäre (z.B. 'drei Monate'), verwende 'for'.

items.p: My sister has played the piano ___ she was six years old.
a: since
items.h: 'She was six years old' is the point when it started. What word means 'from that point until now'?
items.hd: 'Sie war sechs Jahre alt' ist der Punkt, an dem es begann. Welches Wort bedeutet 'von diesem Punkt bis jetzt'?
items.e: 'Since' is used before a clause that shows when something started: since she was six = from age six until now.
items.ed: 'Since' steht vor einem Satz, der zeigt, wann etwas begann: since she was six = seit sie sechs ist bis jetzt.

items.p: Which sentence is correct?
items.c: I have lived here for five years.
a: I have lived here for five years.
ds: I have lived here since five years.
I live here for five years.
I lived here since five years.
items.h: 'Five years' is a duration. Which word goes with duration? And which tense do we need with for/since?
items.hd: 'Fünf Jahre' ist eine Dauer. Welches Wort steht bei Dauer? Und welche Zeitform brauchen wir mit for/since?
items.e: Duration → 'for' (not 'since'). With for/since, always use the present perfect: I have lived here for five years.
items.ed: Dauer → 'for' (nicht 'since'). Mit for/since verwende immer das Present Perfect: I have lived here for five years.

items.p: Which sentence is correct?
items.c: She has worked there since September.
a: She has worked there since September.
ds: She has worked there for September.
items.h: September is a starting point. Which preposition do we use? And what tense is needed?
items.hd: September ist ein Anfangspunkt. Welche Präposition verwenden wir? Und welche Zeitform brauchen wir?
items.e: Starting point → 'since'. The action continues to now → present perfect: She has worked there since September.
items.ed: Anfangspunkt → 'since'. Die Handlung dauert bis jetzt an → Present Perfect: She has worked there since September.

items.p: Which sentence is correct?
items.c: They haven't eaten anything since breakfast.
a: They haven't eaten anything since breakfast.
ds: They haven't eaten anything for breakfast.
They don't eat anything since breakfast.
They didn't eat anything since breakfast.
items.h: 'Breakfast' is when the period of not eating started. Starting point or duration?
items.hd: 'Frühstück' ist der Zeitpunkt, ab dem sie nichts gegessen haben. Anfangspunkt oder Dauer?
items.e: 'Since breakfast' = from breakfast until now (starting point). Must use present perfect: haven't eaten since breakfast.
items.ed: 'Since breakfast' = seit dem Frühstück bis jetzt (Anfangspunkt). Man muss das Present Perfect verwenden: haven't eaten since breakfast.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: I have known her since three years.
items.c: I have known her for three years.
a: I have known her for three years.
I have known her for three years
I've known her for three years.
I've known her for three years
items.h: 'Three years' is a duration (how long). Which word do we use with a duration?
items.hd: 'Drei Jahre' ist eine Dauer (wie lange). Welches Wort verwenden wir bei einer Dauer?
items.e: 'Three years' is a duration → use 'for'. L1 trap: German 'seit drei Jahren' sounds like 'since three years'.
items.ed: 'Drei Jahre' ist eine Dauer → verwende 'for'. L1-Falle: Deutsch 'seit drei Jahren' klingt wie 'since three years'.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: He lives in Salzburg since 2019.
items.c: He has lived in Salzburg since 2019.
a: He has lived in Salzburg since 2019.
He has lived in Salzburg since 2019
He's lived in Salzburg since 2019.
He's lived in Salzburg since 2019
items.h: The action started in 2019 and continues now. Can we use the present simple with 'since'?
items.hd: Die Handlung begann 2019 und dauert jetzt noch an. Können wir das Present Simple mit 'since' verwenden?
items.e: With 'since', you MUST use the present perfect. German uses present tense (Er wohnt seit 2019), but English requires: He has lived since 2019.
items.ed: Mit 'since' MUSS man das Present Perfect verwenden. Deutsch verwendet Präsens (Er wohnt seit 2019), aber Englisch verlangt: He has lived since 2019.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: We are friends for ten years.
items.c: We have been friends for ten years.
a: We have been friends for ten years.
We have been friends for ten years
We've been friends for ten years.
We've been friends for ten years
items.h: The friendship started ten years ago and continues now. Which tense do we need with 'for'?
items.hd: Die Freundschaft begann vor zehn Jahren und dauert bis jetzt. Welche Zeitform brauchen wir mit 'for'?
items.e: With 'for' + duration, use the present perfect: We have been friends for ten years. German uses present tense (Wir sind seit zehn Jahren Freunde).
items.ed: Mit 'for' + Dauer verwende das Present Perfect: We have been friends for ten years. Deutsch verwendet Präsens (Wir sind seit zehn Jahren Freunde).

items.p: Your exchange student asks how long you've studied English. Answer in one sentence: 'I ________ (learn) English ________ 2019.'
a: have learnt ... since
have learned ... since
have learnt, since
have learned, since
've learnt ... since
've learned ... since
items.h: 2019 is a point in time. Which word goes with a point in time: for or since?
items.hd: 2019 ist ein Zeitpunkt. Welches Wort passt zu einem Zeitpunkt: for oder since?
items.e: 'Since' is used with a point in time (2019). Present perfect + since shows an action that started in the past and continues now.
items.ed: 'Since' wird mit einem Zeitpunkt (2019) verwendet. Present perfect + since zeigt eine Handlung, die in der Vergangenheit begann und jetzt weitergeht.

items.p: You're writing a message to a new classmate about your best friend. Complete: 'She ________ (be) my best friend ________ three years.'
a: has been ... for
has been, for
items.h: 'Three years' is a duration. Which word goes with a duration: for or since?
items.hd: 'Three years' ist eine Zeitdauer. Welches Wort passt zu einer Zeitdauer: for oder since?
items.e: 'For' is used with a duration (three years). 'She has been my best friend for three years.'
items.ed: 'For' wird mit einer Zeitdauer (three years) verwendet. 'She has been my best friend for three years.'

items.p: Tell your pen pal about your home: 'We ________ (live) here ________ September.'
a: have lived ... since
have lived, since
've lived ... since
items.h: 'September' is a point in time (a specific month). For or since?
items.hd: 'September' ist ein Zeitpunkt (ein bestimmter Monat). For oder since?
items.e: 'Since' is used with a point in time (September). 'We have lived here since September.'
items.ed: 'Since' wird mit einem Zeitpunkt (September) verwendet. 'We have lived here since September.'

items.p: 🇩🇪 Ich wohne seit fuenf Jahren in Graz.
items.c: I have lived in Graz for five years.
a: I have lived in Graz for five years.
I've lived in Graz for five years.
I have lived in Graz for five years
I've lived in Graz for five years
items.h: German uses present tense with 'seit', but English needs present perfect. Is 'fuenf Jahre' a duration or a starting point?
items.hd: Deutsch verwendet Präsens mit 'seit', aber Englisch braucht Present Perfect. Ist 'fünf Jahre' eine Dauer oder ein Anfangspunkt?
items.e: German 'Ich wohne seit...' = present tense, but English requires present perfect: I have lived. 'Fuenf Jahre' is a duration → 'for'.
items.ed: Deutsch 'Ich wohne seit...' = Präsens, aber Englisch verlangt Present Perfect: I have lived. 'Fünf Jahre' ist eine Dauer → 'for'.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Er spielt seit Montag kein Fussball.
items.c: He hasn't played football since Monday.
a: He hasn't played football since Monday.
He has not played football since Monday.
He hasn't played football since Monday
He has not played football since Monday
items.h: 'Seit Montag' = since Monday (starting point). Don't forget: English needs present perfect, not present tense!
items.hd: 'Seit Montag' = since Monday (Anfangspunkt). Vergiss nicht: Englisch braucht Present Perfect, nicht Präsens!
items.e: German present tense 'spielt...kein' with 'seit' → English present perfect negative: hasn't played since Monday.
items.ed: Deutsch Präsens 'spielt...kein' mit 'seit' → Englisch Present Perfect Verneinung: hasn't played since Monday.

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: for / lived / three / they / here / years / have
items.c: They have lived here for three years.
a: They have lived here for three years.
They have lived here for three years
items.h: Subject + have + past participle + place + for + duration.
items.hd: Subjekt + have + Partizip + Ort + for + Dauer.
items.e: Word order: They have lived here for three years. Subject + have + past participle + for + duration.
items.ed: Wortstellung: They have lived here for three years. Subjekt + have + Partizip + for + Dauer.

items.p:
Match each time expression with 'for' or 'since':
1. ___ two hours
2. ___ last summer
3. ___ a long time
4. ___ 2020
5. ___ three weeks
6. ___ I was a child
a. for
b. since
items.h: Duration (how long) → for. Starting point (when it began) → since.
items.hd: Dauer (wie lange) → for. Anfangspunkt (wann es begann) → since.
items.e: For + duration: two hours, a long time, three weeks. Since + starting point: last summer, 2020, I was a child.
items.ed: For + Dauer: two hours, a long time, three weeks. Since + Anfangspunkt: last summer, 2020, I was a child.

items.p: Tom moved to Vienna in 2020. He still lives there. Which sentence is correct?
items.c: Tom has lived in Vienna since 2020.
a: Tom has lived in Vienna since 2020.
ds: Tom has lived in Vienna since six years.
Tom has lived in Vienna for 2020.
items.h: Tom moved in 2020 and STILL lives there. Which tense + time word combination shows an action continuing from a past point to now?
items.hd: Tom zog 2020 um und lebt NOCH dort. Welche Zeitform + Zeitwort-Kombination zeigt eine Handlung, die von einem vergangenen Zeitpunkt bis jetzt andauert?
items.e: Present perfect + 'since' + point in time (2020) shows an action that started in the past and continues now.
items.ed: Present perfect + 'since' + Zeitpunkt (2020) zeigt eine Handlung, die in der Vergangenheit begann und jetzt andauert.

items.p: I ___ (go) to the cinema yesterday.
a: went
items.h: 'Yesterday' is a specific time in the past. Which tense do we use with specific past times?
items.hd: 'Yesterday' ist ein bestimmter Zeitpunkt in der Vergangenheit. Welche Zeitform verwenden wir bei bestimmten vergangenen Zeitpunkten?
items.e: 'Yesterday' = specific past time → past simple. I went to the cinema yesterday. Not present perfect!
items.ed: 'Yesterday' = bestimmter Zeitpunkt in der Vergangenheit → Simple Past. I went to the cinema yesterday. Nicht Present Perfect!

items.p: ___ you ever ___ (be) to London?
a: Have ... been
Have...been
Have been
items.h: 'Ever' is a signal word for experience. Which tense do we use for life experience?
items.hd: 'Ever' ist ein Signalwort für Erfahrungen. Welche Zeitform verwenden wir für Lebenserfahrungen?
items.e: 'Ever' = life experience → present perfect. Have you ever been to London? (not 'Did you ever be...')
items.ed: 'Ever' = Lebenserfahrung → Present Perfect. Have you ever been to London? (nicht 'Did you ever be...')

items.p: She ___ (just / finish) her homework. She can play now.
a: has just finished
's just finished
items.h: 'Just' means a very short time ago, and the result is important NOW. Which tense shows this?
items.hd: 'Just' bedeutet vor sehr kurzer Zeit, und das Ergebnis ist JETZT wichtig. Welche Zeitform zeigt das?
items.e: 'Just' = very recently, relevant now → present perfect: She has just finished. Subject + has/have + just + past participle.
items.ed: 'Just' = gerade eben, jetzt relevant → Present Perfect: She has just finished. Subjekt + has/have + just + Partizip.

items.p: We ___ (visit) Paris three times. We love it!
a: have visited
've visited
items.h: No specific past time is mentioned. We're talking about experience (how many times). Which tense?
items.hd: Kein bestimmter vergangener Zeitpunkt wird genannt. Wir sprechen über Erfahrung (wie oft). Welche Zeitform?
items.e: No specific time + life experience = present perfect. We have visited Paris three times.
items.ed: Kein bestimmter Zeitpunkt + Lebenserfahrung = Present Perfect. We have visited Paris three times.

items.p: My family ___ (move) to Linz in 2015.
a: moved
items.h: 'In 2015' is a specific time. Which tense do we use for finished actions at a specific time?
items.hd: 'In 2015' ist ein bestimmter Zeitpunkt. Welche Zeitform verwenden wir für abgeschlossene Handlungen zu einem bestimmten Zeitpunkt?
items.e: 'In 2015' = specific past time → past simple: My family moved to Linz in 2015.
items.ed: 'In 2015' = bestimmter vergangener Zeitpunkt → Simple Past: My family moved to Linz in 2015.

items.p: I ___ (not / eat) sushi yet. Is it good?
a: haven't eaten
have not eaten
items.h: 'Yet' is a signal word. It always goes with which tense? And remember: have + past participle!
items.hd: 'Yet' ist ein Signalwort. Es steht immer bei welcher Zeitform? Und denke daran: have + Partizip!
items.e: 'Yet' = present perfect signal word. Negative: haven't/hasn't + past participle. I haven't eaten (not 'ate') sushi yet.
items.ed: 'Yet' = Present Perfect Signalwort. Verneinung: haven't/hasn't + Partizip. I haven't eaten (nicht 'ate') sushi yet.

items.p: Which sentence is correct?
items.c: I saw that film last week.
a: I saw that film last week.
ds: I have seen that film last week.
I have saw that film last week.
I did see that film last week.
items.h: 'Last week' is a specific past time. Which tense goes with specific past times?
items.hd: 'Last week' ist ein bestimmter vergangener Zeitpunkt. Welche Zeitform steht bei bestimmten vergangenen Zeitpunkten?
items.e: 'Last week' = specific past time → past simple: I saw that film last week. Never use present perfect with 'last week'.
items.ed: 'Last week' = bestimmter vergangener Zeitpunkt → Simple Past: I saw that film last week. Verwende niemals Present Perfect mit 'last week'.

items.p: Which sentence is correct?
items.c: She has already done her homework.
a: She has already done her homework.
ds: She has already did her homework.
She already has done her homework.
items.h: 'Already' is a present perfect signal word. Where does it go in the sentence?
items.hd: 'Already' ist ein Present Perfect Signalwort. Wo steht es im Satz?
items.e: Already → present perfect. Word order: has + already + past participle. She has already done her homework.
items.ed: Already → Present Perfect. Wortstellung: has + already + Partizip. She has already done her homework.

items.p: Which sentence is correct?
a: I have never tried snowboarding.
items.h: 'Never' talks about life experience (from birth until now). Which tense do we use?
items.hd: 'Never' spricht über Lebenserfahrung (von Geburt bis jetzt). Welche Zeitform verwenden wir?
items.e: 'Never' = life experience → present perfect. I have never tried snowboarding. Have + never + past participle.
items.ed: 'Never' = Lebenserfahrung → Present Perfect. I have never tried snowboarding. Have + never + Partizip.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: I have been to the zoo yesterday.
items.c: I went to the zoo yesterday.
a: I went to the zoo yesterday.
I went to the zoo yesterday
I was at the zoo yesterday.
I was at the zoo yesterday.
items.h: 'Yesterday' is a specific past time. Can we use present perfect with 'yesterday'?
items.hd: 'Yesterday' ist ein bestimmter vergangener Zeitpunkt. Können wir Present Perfect mit 'yesterday' verwenden?
items.e: Never use present perfect with 'yesterday'. Yesterday = specific past time → past simple: I went to the zoo yesterday.
items.ed: Verwende niemals Present Perfect mit 'yesterday'. Yesterday = bestimmter vergangener Zeitpunkt → Simple Past: I went to the zoo yesterday.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: Did you ever visit Rome?
a: Have you ever visited Rome?
Have you ever visited Rome
Have you ever been to Rome?
Have you ever been to Rome
items.h: 'Ever' is about life experience. Which tense do we use for experience questions?
items.hd: 'Ever' bezieht sich auf Lebenserfahrung. Welche Zeitform verwenden wir für Erfahrungsfragen?
items.e: 'Ever' = life experience → present perfect. Have you ever visited/been to Rome? Not 'Did you ever visit...'
items.ed: 'Ever' = Lebenserfahrung → Present Perfect. Have you ever visited/been to Rome? Nicht 'Did you ever visit...'

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: She already finished the book two days ago.
items.c: She finished the book two days ago.
a: She finished the book two days ago.
She finished the book two days ago
She has already finished the book.
items.h: 'Two days ago' = specific past time. 'Already' = present perfect signal. These two don't go together!
items.hd: 'Two days ago' = bestimmter vergangener Zeitpunkt. 'Already' = Present Perfect Signalwort. Die beiden passen nicht zusammen!
items.e: You can't mix past simple signals (ago) with present perfect signals (already). Choose one: 'She finished it two days ago.' OR 'She has already finished it.'
items.ed: Man kann Simple Past Signalwörter (ago) nicht mit Present Perfect Signalwörtern (already) mischen. Wähle eins: 'She finished it two days ago.' ODER 'She has already finished it.'

items.p: Your friend recommends a film. You've already seen it but you don't say when. Tell your friend: 'I ________ already ________ (see) that film.'
a: have ... seen
have already seen
've ... seen
've already seen
items.h: You've seen the film at some point in your life (no specific time). Which tense is for life experiences?
items.hd: Du hast den Film irgendwann in deinem Leben gesehen (kein bestimmter Zeitpunkt). Welche Zeitform ist für Lebenserfahrungen?
items.e: Present perfect with 'already' for a life experience without a specific time: 'I have already seen that film.'
items.ed: Present perfect mit 'already' für eine Lebenserfahrung ohne bestimmten Zeitpunkt: 'I have already seen that film.'

items.p: You're writing in your diary about last summer. Complete the entry: 'Last summer, I ________ (visit) London with my family.'
a: visited
items.h: 'Last summer' is a specific, finished time in the past. Which tense is for completed actions at a specific past time?
items.hd: 'Last summer' ist ein bestimmter, abgeschlossener Zeitpunkt in der Vergangenheit. Welche Zeitform ist für abgeschlossene Handlungen zu einem bestimmten vergangenen Zeitpunkt?
items.e: Past simple for a completed action at a specific past time: 'Last summer, I visited London.'
items.ed: Past simple für eine abgeschlossene Handlung zu einem bestimmten vergangenen Zeitpunkt: 'Last summer, I visited London.'

items.p: Your host family asks if you've tried Austrian food. Answer about your experience: 'I ________ never ________ (try) Kaiserschmarren.'
a: have ... tried
have never tried
've ... tried
've never tried
items.h: This is about your life experience (ever/never). Which tense is for experiences up to now?
items.hd: Es geht um deine Lebenserfahrung (ever/never). Welche Zeitform ist für Erfahrungen bis jetzt?
items.e: Present perfect with 'never' for life experiences: 'I have never tried Kaiserschmarren.' No specific time is given.
items.ed: Present perfect mit 'never' für Lebenserfahrungen: 'I have never tried Kaiserschmarren.' Kein bestimmter Zeitpunkt wird genannt.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Ich habe diesen Film schon gesehen.
items.c: I have already seen this film.
a: I have already seen this film.
I've already seen this film.
I have already seen this movie.
I've already seen this movie.
I have already seen this film
I've already seen this film
items.h: 'Schon' = already. German Perfekt here → English present perfect (no specific time, relevant now).
items.hd: 'Schon' = already. Deutsches Perfekt hier → Englisches Present Perfect (kein bestimmter Zeitpunkt, jetzt relevant).
items.e: German Perfekt + 'schon' → English present perfect + 'already': I have already seen this film.
items.ed: Deutsches Perfekt + 'schon' → Englisches Present Perfect + 'already': I have already seen this film.

items.p: 🇩🇪 Wir sind letzten Sommer nach Italien gefahren.
items.c: We went to Italy last summer.
a: We went to Italy last summer.
We went to Italy last summer
We travelled to Italy last summer.
We traveled to Italy last summer.
items.h: 'Letzten Sommer' = last summer = specific past time. German Perfekt does NOT always become English present perfect!
items.hd: 'Letzten Sommer' = last summer = bestimmter vergangener Zeitpunkt. Deutsches Perfekt wird NICHT immer zu englischem Present Perfect!
items.e: German Perfekt + specific time (letzten Sommer) → English PAST SIMPLE: We went to Italy last summer. Not present perfect!
items.ed: Deutsches Perfekt + bestimmter Zeitpunkt (letzten Sommer) → Englisches SIMPLE PAST: We went to Italy last summer. Nicht Present Perfect!

items.p: Put the words in the correct order: never / I / been / have / to / Spain
items.c: I have never been to Spain.
a: I have never been to Spain.
I have never been to Spain
items.h: Subject + have + never + past participle + rest of sentence.
items.hd: Subjekt + have + never + Partizip + Rest des Satzes.
items.e: Word order with 'never': I have never been to Spain. Subject + have + never + past participle.
items.ed: Wortstellung mit 'never': I have never been to Spain. Subjekt + have + never + Partizip.

items.p:
Match each signal word with the correct tense:
1. yesterday
2. ever
3. last week
4. already
5. ago
6. just
a. past simple
b. present perfect
items.h: Think: does the signal word point to a specific past time, or to experience/result now?
items.hd: Überlege: Zeigt das Signalwort auf einen bestimmten vergangenen Zeitpunkt oder auf Erfahrung/Ergebnis jetzt?
items.e: Past simple: yesterday, last week, ago (specific past time). Present perfect: ever, already, just (experience/result now).
items.ed: Simple Past: yesterday, last week, ago (bestimmter vergangener Zeitpunkt). Present Perfect: ever, already, just (Erfahrung/Ergebnis jetzt).

items.p: Your friend asks about your weekend. You went to the cinema yesterday. Which sentence is correct?
items.c: I went to the cinema yesterday.
a: I went to the cinema yesterday.
ds: I have gone to the cinema yesterday.
I go to the cinema yesterday.
I was going to the cinema yesterday.
items.h: 'Yesterday' is a specific, finished time. Which tense is for completed actions at a specific past time?
items.hd: 'Yesterday' ist ein bestimmter, abgeschlossener Zeitpunkt. Welche Zeitform ist für abgeschlossene Handlungen zu einem bestimmten vergangenen Zeitpunkt?
items.e: Past simple is used for completed actions at a specific past time. 'Yesterday' = specific time, so use past simple.
items.ed: Past simple wird für abgeschlossene Handlungen zu einem bestimmten vergangenen Zeitpunkt verwendet. 'Yesterday' = bestimmter Zeitpunkt, also past simple.

items.p: We ___ allowed to use our phones in class.
a: aren't
are not
items.h: Which form of 'be' matches 'we' in the present?
items.hd: Welche Form von 'be' passt zu 'we' im Präsens?
items.e: 'We' takes 'are' in the present, so the negative is 'aren't allowed to'. Remember: be + allowed to needs a form of 'be'.
items.ed: 'We' braucht 'are' im Präsens, also ist die Verneinung 'aren't allowed to'. Denk daran: be + allowed to braucht eine Form von 'be'.

items.p: ___ you allowed to stay up late on Fridays?
a: Are
items.h: Questions with 'allowed to' start with a form of 'be'. Which form goes with 'you'?
items.hd: Fragen mit 'allowed to' beginnen mit einer Form von 'be'. Welche Form passt zu 'you'?
items.e: 'Be allowed to' questions need a form of 'be' at the start. 'You' takes 'are', so: 'Are you allowed to...?'
items.ed: Fragen mit 'be allowed to' brauchen eine Form von 'be' am Anfang. 'You' braucht 'are', also: 'Are you allowed to...?'

items.p: She ___ allowed to go to the concert last weekend.
a: wasn't
was not
items.h: 'Last weekend' tells you the tense. Which past form of 'be' goes with 'she'?
items.hd: 'Last weekend' zeigt dir die Zeitform. Welche Vergangenheitsform von 'be' passt zu 'she'?
items.e: 'Last weekend' signals past tense. 'She' takes 'was' in the past, so the negative is 'wasn't allowed to'.
items.ed: 'Last weekend' zeigt Vergangenheit. 'She' braucht 'was' in der Vergangenheit, also ist die Verneinung 'wasn't allowed to'.

items.p: At my old school, we ___ allowed to wear jeans.
a: were
items.h: 'At my old school' shows it happened in the past. Which past form of 'be' goes with 'we'?
items.hd: 'At my old school' zeigt, dass es in der Vergangenheit war. Welche Vergangenheitsform von 'be' passt zu 'we'?
items.e: 'At my old school' means the past. 'We' takes 'were' in the past: 'We were allowed to wear jeans.'
items.ed: 'At my old school' bedeutet Vergangenheit. 'We' braucht 'were' in der Vergangenheit: 'We were allowed to wear jeans.'

items.p: Tom ___ allowed to borrow the car because he passed his driving test.
a: is
's
items.h: 'Tom' is 3rd person singular. Which form of 'be' matches?
items.hd: 'Tom' ist 3. Person Einzahl. Welche Form von 'be' passt?
items.e: 'Tom' is 3rd person singular, so we use 'is allowed to'. We use 'be allowed to' (not 'can') because someone gave him permission.
items.ed: 'Tom' ist 3. Person Einzahl, also verwenden wir 'is allowed to'. Wir verwenden 'be allowed to' (nicht 'can'), weil ihm jemand die Erlaubnis gegeben hat.

items.p: When I was little, I ___ allowed to watch TV after 9 pm, but now I ___ .
a: wasn't ... am
was not ... am
items.h: Both gaps need a form of 'be'. The first is past ('when I was little'), the second is present ('now').
items.hd: Beide Lücken brauchen eine Form von 'be'. Die erste ist Vergangenheit ('when I was little'), die zweite ist Gegenwart ('now').
items.e: Past: 'I wasn't allowed to' (was + not). Present: 'I am (allowed to)'. 'Be allowed to' always uses 'be', never 'do'.
items.ed: Vergangenheit: 'I wasn't allowed to' (was + not). Gegenwart: 'I am (allowed to)'. 'Be allowed to' verwendet immer 'be', nie 'do'.

items.p: Which sentence is correct?
items.c: We aren't allowed to run in the corridor.
a: We aren't allowed to run in the corridor.
ds: We don't allowed to run in the corridor.
We not allowed to run in the corridor.
We aren't allowed run in the corridor.
items.h: The structure is: be (not) allowed to + base verb.
items.hd: Die Struktur ist: be (not) allowed to + Grundform.
items.e: The correct structure is 'aren't allowed to run'. We need 'be' for negation (not 'do'), and 'to' after 'allowed'.
items.ed: Die richtige Struktur ist 'aren't allowed to run'. Wir brauchen 'be' für die Verneinung (nicht 'do') und 'to' nach 'allowed'.

items.p: Choose the correct option: My little brother ___ to play with matches.
a: isn't allowed
items.h: 'My little brother' is 3rd person singular. Which form of 'be' + 'not' fits?
items.hd: 'My little brother' ist 3. Person Einzahl. Welche Form von 'be' + 'not' passt?
items.e: 'My little brother' = 'he', so we need 'isn't allowed to'. 'Be allowed to' always uses a form of 'be', never 'do/does'.
items.ed: 'My little brother' = 'he', also brauchen wir 'isn't allowed to'. 'Be allowed to' verwendet immer eine Form von 'be', nie 'do/does'.

items.p: Which sentence correctly uses 'be allowed to' for permission?
items.c: You're not allowed to take photos in the museum.
a: You're not allowed to take photos in the museum.
ds: You're not allowed taking photos in the museum.
You don't allow to take photos in the museum.
You not allowed to take photos in the museum.
items.h: The structure is: subject + be (not) + allowed + to + base verb.
items.hd: Die Struktur ist: Subjekt + be (not) + allowed + to + Grundform.
items.e: 'You're not allowed to take' has all the right elements: be + not + allowed + to + base verb.
items.ed: 'You're not allowed to take' hat alle richtigen Elemente: be + not + allowed + to + Grundform.

items.p: Find and fix the mistake: I not allowed to eat sweets before dinner.
items.c: I'm not allowed to eat sweets before dinner.
a: I'm not allowed to eat sweets before dinner.
I am not allowed to eat sweets before dinner.
I'm not allowed to eat sweets before dinner
items.h: Something is missing before 'not allowed'. What verb do you always need with 'allowed to'?
items.hd: Vor 'not allowed' fehlt etwas. Welches Verb brauchst du immer bei 'allowed to'?
